front: Artist: Danish-American muckraking photographer; exposé of New York tenements subtitled 'Studies among the Tenements of New York'
back: Jacob Riis (How the Other Half Lives)

front: Photograph: Shows at least ten threatening men standing in a back alley at 59½ Mulberry Street, New York
Photograph: Shows lodgers crammed into a dirty bedroom on Bayard Street; subtitled 'Five Cents a Spot'
work: Five Cents a Spot / Lodgers in Bayard Street (1889)

front: Artist: Pioneered indoor flash photography using magnesium powder; once accidentally lit himself on fire
Artist: Theodore Roosevelt read this artist's book and wrote 'I have read your book, and I have come to help'; called him 'the best American I ever knew'
Artist: Photographed gangs of street boys he called 'Street Arabs'; clotheslines in his photos represented the 'poverty line'
Artist: His book led to the demolition of the Mulberry Bend slum and creation of a park in the Five Points neighborhood
back: Jacob Riis (How the Other Half Lives)
